comments.comment_text:
🌟 IRAN: FACTS RARELY DISCUSSED IN WESTERN MEDIA
- When many Westerners hear “Iran,” they imagine a fanatic government that wants to harm Israelis or Americans.
- Iran is not a perfect society, and many Iranians themselves criticize aspects of their government. But the reality is more complex than the stereotypes often presented in Western media.
- Here are some important facts that are rarely discussed.
✅ RELIGIOUS MINORITIES
- Iran officially recognizes Judaism, Christianity, and Zoroastrianism as protected religions.
- These minorities have reserved seats in parliament:
     - 1 Jewish
     - 1 Zoroastrian
     - 3 Christian (Armenian and Assyrian communities)
🕍 JEWISH COMMUNITY
- Iran has the second-largest Jewish population in the Middle East outside Israel.
- There are 13 synagogues in the city of Isfahan alone.
- Jews have lived in Persia continuously for more than 2,500 years, dating back to the Persian Empire after the Babylonian exile.
✝️ CHRISTIAN HISTORY
- Christian communities have existed in Persia since the 1st–3rd centuries CE.
- Armenian and Assyrian Christian communities remain today.
- Many churches belong to the historic Church of the East tradition.
👩 WOMEN IN IRAN
- Iranian women face some legal inequalities, as women do in many countries.
- But they drive, travel, study, work, and participate widely in public life.
- Women are highly educated and often make up a majority of university graduates in science and engineering, contributing strongly to medicine, research, and technology.
🕊 IRAN & ISRAEL / PALESTINE
- Iran’s opposition to Israel is political and based largely on the 1948 displacement of Palestinians (the Nakba) and the continuing conflict over Palestinian rights.
- Iranian leaders frequently emphasize opposition to Israeli government policies and the occupation of Palestinian territories rather than hostility toward Jewish people as a religion.
🌍 GLOBAL SOLIDARITY
- South Africa: Iran supported the anti-apartheid struggle and the African National Congress. The United States, United Kingdom, and Israel maintained various forms of cooperation with apartheid South Africa for many years.
- Ireland: Many people in Ireland express solidarity with Palestinians because they also experienced great suffering due to European colonization, including the devastation of the Great Famine (Ireland) in the 19th century.
- Humanitarian cooperation: Iran has provided fuel, medicine, and technical assistance to countries facing heavy sanctions such as Venezuela and Cuba, including help maintaining medical equipment and energy infrastructure.
📊 THREE SURPRISING FACTS
- Iran reserves 5 parliamentary seats for religious minorities.
- Jews have lived in Persia for over 2,500 years, with 13 synagogues in Isfahan alone.
- Women often make up a majority of science and engineering graduates.
⚠️ WHY THIS MATTERS
- Understanding other countries accurately is important — especially when politicians talk about sanctions, confrontation, or war.
- No nation of nearly 90 million people with thousands of years of civilization can be understood through simple stereotypes.
❓ QUESTION
- Why do you think these facts are rarely mentioned in Western discussions about Iran?

comments.comment_text:
🚨 Day 10 of my hunger strike. Please read🙏🏽
Future generations will ask what we did.
Ordinary people can still stop catastrophic wars.

I’m an American on Day 10 without food (240+ hours) to try to stop the war with Iran before it spirals into something far worse.

Because somewhere tonight, a parent may only be able to identify their child by a backpack or a shoe after an airstrike.

I refuse to stay silent while another devastating war spreads.

What I’m asking you to do will take about 2 minutes.

If even 1 in 20 people who see this act, thousands of messages will reach Congress today.

PLEASE help me demand: NO escalation of the war with Iran

Tell Congress:
– Support the War Powers Resolution (H.Con.Res. 38 / S.J.Res. 104)
– No unauthorized war with Iran
– Immediate de-escalation
– Unimpeded aid into Gaza
– Access for international journalists, humanitarian organizations, and observers into Gaza, the West Bank, and Lebanon
Civilians across the region deserve protection, food, medicine, and peace.

It takes about 2 minutes

📞 Calling is the most effective
- Call your representatives (see exact script in Comments!)
www.5calls.org/issue/iran-israel-war-us-involvement/⁠�
- Email Congress 
www.democracy.io⁠�
- Or text RESIST to 50409 (ResistBot will guide you)

Why I’m doing this

Before supporting another war, Americans should remember a few things.
– The Iraq war was justified with claims about weapons of mass destruction that were never found.
– The result: tens of thousands of American service members killed or injured, trillions of dollars added to the national debt, and millions of Iraqis killed, injured, or displaced.
- For decades, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has repeatedly warned that Iran was “months away” from nuclear weapons.
Those warnings have been made for more than 30 years.

A simple moral truth

Every innocent life matters — Israeli, Palestinian, Iranian, Lebanese, American, and everyone else.
No child anywhere deserves to die in war.

The humanitarian reality

- Since the first months of Israel’s bombing in October–November 2023, Gaza has faced a grave humanitarian catastrophe.
- Entire communities have been flattened and millions displaced.
- Around 70,000 Palestinians have been killed in Gaza and more than 170,000 injured, according to figures health authorities report and Israeli officials have said are broadly accurate.

- In Lebanon, more than a million people were displaced during the fighting.
- Public health researchers note that in modern wars many more people often die indirectly than from bombs or bullets — from hunger, disease, lack of clean water, and the collapse of hospitals and infrastructure.
- The true human toll of war is often far higher than the initial death counts reported during the fighting.

What escalation could mean

- Hundreds of thousands of civilians have already been killed or injured across the region.
- If the war with Iran continues expanding, tens of thousands more innocent civilians could die.
- History shows wars often grow far beyond what leaders predict.

My small sacrifice

- I haven’t eaten in more than 150 hours.
- But that is nothing compared to the suffering of civilians caught in these conflicts.
- I will go to my grave wishing I had done more to stop unnecessary violence.

So this is one small thing I can do.
And I’m asking you to help.

If you’re not American
Contact your members of parliament or national representatives and ask them to support:
– De-escalation
– Protection of civilians
– Humanitarian access
– Peace and diplomacy
And connect with others who support human rights and international law.

One last request

- My goal is to get at least 100 people to take action today.
- Take one simple step: contact Congress (or your representatives) and then comment “DONE” so others can see the momentum.
- If you can’t call today, please SHARE this post.
- If just 1 in 20 people shares it, thousands more people will see it today.

Silence is how wars expand.

Public pressure is how they stop.

🙏🏽 Please share.
- I may only be one person refusing food.
- But together we can still refuse another unnecessary war.

comments.comment_text:
U.S. Flag Code — Conduct During the National Anthem

36 U.S.C. §301(b)(1)

During a rendition of the national anthem—

(1) when the flag is displayed—

(A) individuals in uniform should give the military salute at the first note of the anthem and maintain that position until the last note;

(B) members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute in the manner provided for individuals in uniform; and

(C) all other persons present should face the flag and stand at attention with their right hand over the heart, and men not in uniform, if applicable, should remove their headdress with their right hand and hold it at the left shoulder, the hand being over the heart.

Citation: 36 U.S.C. §301(b)(1)


---

U.S. Flag Code — Conduct During Hoisting, Lowering, or Passing of the Flag

36 U.S.C. §301(b)(2)

During the ceremony of hoisting or lowering the flag or when the flag is passing in a parade or in review, all persons present except those in uniform should face the flag and stand at attention with the right hand over the heart. Those present in uniform should render the military salute. Members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute.

Citation: 36 U.S.C. §301(b)(2)


Before the 2008–2009 amendments, civilians were expected to:

• remove hats
• place the right hand over the heart

The amendment created a special exception for veterans and active service members not in uniform, allowing them to:

• give the military salute instead
• perform it the same way as someone in uniform

36 U.S.C. §301(b)(1)(B)

Members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute in the manner provided for individuals in uniform.

comments.author: @SAR0311
comments.video_title: Fox News CAUGHT Covering Up Trump Disgrace At Fallen Soldier Arrival Ceremony
comments.comment_text:
1. Current U.S. Code (36 U.S.C. §301(b)(1))

During a rendition of the national anthem—

(1) when the flag is displayed—

(A) individuals in uniform should give the military salute at the first note of the anthem and maintain that position until the last note;

(B) members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute in the manner provided for individuals in uniform; and

(C) all other persons present should face the flag and stand at attention with their right hand over the heart, and men not in uniform, if applicable, should remove their headdress with their right hand and hold it at the left shoulder, the hand being over the heart.

Citation: 36 U.S.C. §301(b)(1)


---

2. National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2009

Public Law 110-417 — Section 595

SEC. 595. MILITARY SALUTE FOR THE FLAG DURING THE NATIONAL ANTHEM BY MEMBERS OF THE ARMED FORCES NOT IN UNIFORM AND BY VETERANS.

Section 301(b)(1) of title 36, United States Code, is amended by striking subparagraphs (A) through (C) and inserting the following new subparagraphs:

(A) Individuals in uniform should give the military salute at the first note of the anthem and maintain that position until the last note;

(B) Members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute in the manner provided for individuals in uniform; and

(C) All other persons present should face the flag and stand at attention with their right hand over the heart, and men not in uniform, if applicable, should remove their headdress with their right hand and hold it at the left shoulder, the hand being over the heart.

Citation: Public Law 110-417, National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2009, Section 595.


---

3. National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2008

Public Law 110-181

This earlier law allowed veterans and service members not in uniform to salute the flag during the raising, lowering, or passing of the flag.

Relevant language:

Members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute during the hoisting, lowering, or passing of the flag.

Citation: Public Law 110-181, National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2008.


---

36 U.S.C. §301(b)(1)(B)

Members of the Armed Forces and veterans who are present but not in uniform may render the military salute in the manner provided for individuals in uniform.
